slides.title: The Copy Problem (Again)
slides.content:
## Methods Copy Too

By default, if you have a `User` struct with a `(u User) Birthday()` method, Go strictly makes a *copy* of the User before running `Birthday()`.

If the `Birthday()` method tries to cleanly do `u.Age = u.Age + 1`, only the temporary copy gets older! Your original User stays the exact same age.

quizData.question: A standard Method cleanly containing a value-receiver `(u User)` correctly seamlessly modifies the original explicitly declared Struct actively effectively in memory perfectly.
quizData.explanation: Value receivers pass entire structs strictly distinctly expressly firmly by completely cloning them firmly natively; original modifications fundamentally securely fail securely.

slides.title: Pointer Receivers to the Rescue 🦸‍♂️
slides.content:
To solve this uniquely, we cleverly change the Receiver securely from `User` to explicitly `*User` (a Pointer receiver).

Now, the Method confidently changes the original struct!

quizData.question: Which Receiver comprehensively guarantees your explicitly natively skillfully securely intelligently expertly explicitly cleverly cleverly safely seamlessly safely seamlessly seamlessly seamlessly properly seamlessly perfectly logically logically smartly smartly securely correctly creatively deftly gracefully efficiently method safely changes the original securely?
quizData.explanation: Pointer Receivers intelligently ensure the method natively expertly elegantly smoothly directly alters the mapped original cleanly.
